title: Pressing handmade sheets without a book press
subtitle: Improvised pressing equipment for home papermakers
excerpt: A commercial papermaking press is not necessary for producing good handmade sheets. Weight-based pressing using boards, clamps, and whatever is heavy in the house works well at home scale. This article covers the pressing sequence, what materials to use for boards, and how long to leave sheets under weight for different fibre types.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943, public domain via Internet Archive), on the pressing of handmade sheets in pre-industrial papermaking using weighted boards and stone.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stack of alternating couching cloths and newly formed sheets, built up after each pull and pressed together as a unit to remove water from the whole batch.

The absorbent cloths or blankets used to interleave with wet sheets in a post. Commercial papermaking uses woollen felts; at home scale, cotton canvas, old blankets, or purpose-made couching cloths serve the same function.
The sequence of pressing a post, replacing wet felts with dry ones, and pressing again to extract progressively more water from the sheets.

content.text: Good pressing removes water efficiently without disturbing the fibre in the sheet. A
that is pressed firmly and then allowed to drain under weight overnight loses more water than one pressed hard for an hour and then left uncovered. The quality of the
(their absorbency and thickness) matters as much as the pressing weight.

Cut two pieces of melamine-coated chipboard or plywood slightly larger than your mould (for A4 sheets: 320 x 320 mm is adequate). Melamine or sealed board does not absorb the press water and warps less than unsealed wood. Place the post between the boards and add weight on top: a heavy pot full of water, stacked books in a bag, or a concrete block. Aim for at least 10 kg of distributed weight over an A4 surface. Use G-clamps on the two short edges of the boards to add directional pressure if weight alone is insufficient.

for home-scale production: press for one hour, remove the boards, replace saturated couching cloths with dry ones, re-stack, re-press overnight. After overnight pressing, the sheets are firm enough to peel. Cotton linter needs less pressing than long-bast fibres like kozo or banana; bast fibres hold more water in their longer cell structures and benefit from two cycles before peeling.

Peel the sheet from the couching cloth by lifting one corner gently and peeling back at a low angle. A sheet that tears rather than peels is still too wet: return it to the press. Dry the peeled sheets flat on a smooth surface or board-dry by pressing them against a sealed board with a sponge to flatten them against the surface. Board-drying produces the flattest results; air-drying on a wire rack allows some dimensional movement.
